Q1: I usually tried to spread my study time across the week, aiming for about four to five hours a day. Being an auditory learner, I'd often listen to lecture recordings while doing other things or replay them during my commute. I kept a loose schedule but honestly, my motivation wasn't always there, so some days I'd put in the hours without really feeling connected to the material. I relied heavily on my peer study group to keep me on track.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I'd try to maintain some regularity, but when deadlines approached, everything shifted toward whatever was most urgent. With five online courses running simultaneously, I had to constantly juggle priorities. Some weeks I'd be super consistent, other weeks it was pure chaos depending on which course needed attention most.
Q3: Usually it was either a notification about something due or a message from one of my study partners saying we should check something out. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there were any updates or new discussion posts. The peer support aspect really helped because my friends would remind me to check the platform when I might have otherwise forgotten or procrastinated.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks, logging in became pretty automatic, especially in the mornings with my coffee. The peer interactions really helped cement that habit because I'd want to see if anyone responded to my posts or if there was something new to discuss. It wasn't necessarily exciting, but it became part of my daily routine like checking email. My study group made it feel less like a chore.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I learn best by listening. I watched over 220 minutes of video content and often replayed sections multiple times to really absorb the information. The discussion forums were also valuable because hearing different perspectives from classmates helped concepts click. Having 38 peer interactions throughout the course really enriched my understanding beyond what I could get from materials alone.
Q6: I'll be honest, I only completed about 18% of the assignments even though my scores on what I did submit were pretty good. I tended to skip reading-heavy materials or lengthy PDFs because they don't match my learning style. Some supplementary resources felt redundant after watching the videos, so I'd skim them at best. If something wasn't immediately relevant to an exam or major assignment, it often got deprioritized.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to either the exam or to real understanding of the material. Discussion posts were valuable because the back-and-forth with peers helped solidify concepts. If something was just busywork or felt disconnected from the core learning objectives, my motivation—which is already pretty low—would drop even further.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different. With required ones, there was external pressure pushing me forward even when I didn't feel like it. Optional stuff? If my motivation wasn't there that day, it just didn't happen. I'd tell myself I'd come back to it later, but later rarely came. The required activities at least gave me structure when my internal drive was lacking.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the audio lectures and listened to them on my phone during walks or while cooking. I'd also record myself summarizing concepts and play those back. My study group would meet in person or on video calls to discuss material, and none of that showed up in the LMS. Some of my most productive studying happened completely offline.
Q10: There were definitely times when I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS wouldn't know it. I'd have a lecture playing on repeat while I took handwritten notes, pausing and rewinding on my downloaded copy. Or I'd be in a two-hour study session with my peer group working through problems together. The LMS only saw me log in briefly, but the real learning was happening elsewhere.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd download the video lectures and audio files once, then study from those for days or weeks without touching the LMS again. It was more convenient to have everything on my phone where I could access it anytime. My LMS activity probably looked pretty minimal during those periods even though I was actively engaged with the content.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really tough. The stress was hitting medium levels and my motivation, which is already low, basically tanked. I was juggling five online courses and my extracurriculars, and everything felt overwhelming. Even though I wasn't anxious about it, I just felt depleted and disconnected from the material for a while there.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or had competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars, I'd log into the LMS but just go through the motions. I might click around, maybe skim some discussion posts, but not really absorb anything meaningful. The workload across five courses meant I had to constantly triage, and sometimes this course got the short end of the stick.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have videos playing but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them, just to feel like I was doing something. Those sessions felt hollow—I was technically present in the LMS but mentally checked out. It usually happened when my stress was peaking or my motivation hit rock bottom.
Q15: I postponed assignments pretty regularly, which explains my low completion rate despite having decent scores when I did submit. Part of it was low motivation—I just couldn't muster the energy to start. Part of it was prioritizing other courses or activities. I knew the assignments were important, but in the moment, other things always seemed more urgent or appealing.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the main thing that got me to actually complete work. Without them, I'd probably just keep listening to lectures indefinitely without producing anything. When a deadline approached, my activity would spike dramatically. I'd cram in submissions and discussions in the final hours. It wasn't ideal, but the external pressure was necessary given my motivation issues.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When I got good feedback on assignments, it gave me a temporary boost and made me want to engage more. But if feedback was delayed or felt generic, it reinforced my tendency to deprioritize that course. The peer feedback in discussions was actually more motivating because it felt more immediate and personal.
Q18: The course structure was fairly flexible, which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could fit it around my schedule and learning style. Bad because without rigid structure, my low motivation meant I'd drift. The peer support intervention really helped because it created external accountability that the course structure alone didn't provide.
Q19: The availability of video lectures made engagement way easier for me as an auditory learner. Being able to download materials was huge. What made it harder was when there were lots of reading-based assignments or when the discussion prompts felt too open-ended. I also struggled when there wasn't clear connection between different course components.
Q20: I can totally relate to that pattern. You might be exploring, trying to understand the material, clicking around to figure out what's important. Or you could be stressed and procrastinating by staying busy in the LMS without actually producing work. Sometimes high activity is about anxiety or confusion rather than productive engagement. It's like treading water instead of swimming forward.
Q21: That resonates with my experience somewhat. My exam scores were decent even though my task completion was only around 43%. I did a lot of learning offline—listening to downloaded lectures, studying with peers, processing information in my own way. The LMS doesn't capture all the ways someone can engage with material. Some people are just efficient learners who don't need to click around constantly.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend on pages. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just scrolling mindlessly. They miss offline study, peer discussions, the thinking you do while walking around. They capture the shadow of engagement, not the substance.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to pass. It's when I'm actively thinking about concepts, discussing them with peers, making connections. For me, engagement is more about mental investment than physical presence in a system. Even when my motivation is low, I can still be engaged when something genuinely interests me.
Q24: The LMS would completely miss my peer study sessions, which were honestly the backbone of my learning experience. It wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures or the conversations that helped concepts click. It also can't capture my emotional state—how despite being generally happy, my low motivation and resilience made consistent engagement a real struggle. The human element of learning just doesn't translate to data points.
